content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento. CA 95t! 14•2329 
M. Eli Underwood 
Law Offices 
Redwine and Sherrill 
1950 Market Street 
Riverside, CA 92501-1720 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-12-142a 
Dear Mr. Underwood: 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
November 14, 2012 
This letter supplements our previous letter dated October 10, 2012, ( Underwood Advice 
Letter, No. A-12-142) regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This 
letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the 
Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (111 re Oglesby (1975) I 
FPPC Ops. 71.) We again note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. 
QUESTION 
Do the Act's lobbyist provisions apply if you seek attorneys' fees from the State for legal 
work performed on behalf of a client, which involved a lawsuit to compel the State to open a 
hiking trail in Southern California, considering your previous communications before Senate 
staff members in support of a bill to open the trail? 
CONCLUSION 
Your legal services on behalf of your client will qualify you as a lobbyist only if you 
receive or become entitled to $2,000 or more in a calendar month for direct communications with 
qualifying officials for the purpose of influencing a legislative or administrative action. While 
we express no opinion should you seek attorneys' fees for time spent communicating with 
legislative staff, seeking attorneys' fees for legal services provided in connection to the lawsuit, 
other than time spent communicating with legislative staff, is not compensation for a direct 
communication with qualifying officials and will not trigger the Act's lobbyist provisions. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 IOOO through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Se1:tions 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated . 
.r:::.. 


FACTS 
File No. A-12-142a 
Page No. 2 
The facts relating to your request for advice are more fully described in our previous 
letter, the Underwood Advice Letter, supra. At this time, you have asked us to further consider 
whether the Act's lobbyist provisions may apply should you seek attorneys' fees from the State 
for legal work perfonned on behalf of a client, in connection with a lawsuit to compel the State 
to open a hiking trail in Southern California, considering your previous communications with 
Senate staff members in support of a bill to open the trail. You have further clarified that you 
will not bill your clients, and you will not seek attorney fees from the State, for the time you 
spent communicating with Senate staff members. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 82039 defines a lobbyist as: 
.. Any individual who receives two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more in 
economic consideration in a calendar month, other than reimbursement for 
reasonable travel expenses, or whose principal duties as an employee are, to 
communicate directly or through his or her agents with any elective state official, 
agency official, or legislative official for the purpose of influencing legislative or 
administrative action." 
Regulation 18239 provides two tests for detennining whether an individual is required to 
register as a lobbyist. One test applies to .. in-house" lobbyists, which are individuals who lobby 
only on behalf of their employer, while the other test applies to .. contract" lobbyists, which are 
individuals who lobby on behalf of any person other than their employer. As an attorney, 
activities on behalf of your clients are not ••on the behalf of your employer." Thus, you qualify 
as a lobbyist for communications on your client's behalf only if you qualify as a .. contract" 
lobbyist under Regulation 18329(b), which states: 
.. A lobbyist is an individual who receives or becomes entitled to receive 
$2,000 or more in compensation in any calendar month for engaging in direct 
communication, other than administrative testimony, with one or more qualifying 
officials for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action." 
Regulation 18329(d)(3) defines a "direct communication" as: 
"[A]ppearing as a witness before, talking to (either by telephone or in 
person), corresponding with, or answering questions or inquiries from any 
qualifying official, either personally or through an agent who acts under one's 
direct supervision, control or direction." 


"Compensation" is defined as: 
File No. A-12-142a 
Page No. 3 
"Any economic consideration, other than reimbursement for reasonable 
travel expenses, i.e., expenses for transportation plus a reasonable sum for food 
and lodging." (Regulation 18239(d)(2).) 
Based upon the facts provided, you have billed your clients only for legal services in 
connection with the lawsuit to compel the State to open the hilcing trail. While you do not intend 
to collect your fees from your clients, you do intend to seek attorneys' fees for your billed 
services from the State. However, while your clients paid for your hotel, food, and airfare to 
travel to Sacramento to speak to Senate staff members in support of a bill to open the hiking trail, 
you state that you will not bill your clients for the time spent communicating with Senate staff 
members and will not seek attorneys' fees for this time. 
Nothing in the facts you have provided indicates that the attorneys' fees you seek from 
the State are related to your direct communications before Senate staff members. In fact, you 
have specifically stated that you are not seeking attorneys' fees for the time spent meeting with 
Senate staff members. Accordingly, it does not appear that the attorneys' fees constitute 
compensation for direct communications with qualifying officials and therefore do not trigger the 
Act's lobbyist provisions. Baring any other compensation that may qualify you as a lobbyist and 
so long as payments made for your hotel, food, and airfare were reasonable, meaning they were 
neither e,ctraordinary or lavish, you are not required to register as a lobbyist at this time. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
BGL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
By: 
Brian G. Lau 
Counsel, Legal Division
